summary: Dan Luu systematically reviews empirical studies on static versus dynamic typing, finding that most controlled experiments show very small effects, if any. The studies suffer from serious methodological flaws including trivial toy tasks, student populations, language familiarity bias, and flawed data classification. While some researchers make strong claims about type systems improving code quality, the empirical evidence is weak, limited in scope, and often contradictory, with programmer variance typically dwarfing any language effects.
key_points: Most controlled experiments find minimal differences between static and dynamic typing, with effects smaller than variance between individual programmers

The highly-cited GitHub study by Ray et al. has fundamental flaws including misclassified projects (Qt translation files as TypeScript, shell scripts as Perl) and confuses different language properties
Studies use trivial tasks (median completion times in hundreds of seconds, tasks avoiding loops/recursion as 'too complex') that don't reflect real-world development
Hanenberg's studies show some benefits for static types in specific narrow contexts but withStudent populations and artificially stripped documentation
Case studies on bug-finding (Python-to-Haskell, Erlang static analysis) show some bugs caught but don't establish comparative advantage over other techniques
No studies examine experienced programmers on non-trivial projects with modern tooling, maintenance tasks, or familiar codebases
money_quotes: I think programmers who doubt that type systems help are basically the tech equivalent of an anti-vaxxer.
These are C++ projects. So the intermediate result appears to not be that TypeScript is reliable, but that projects mis-identified as TypeScript are reliable.
The variance between programmers was still much greater than the variance between languages in all but two tasks.
If you look at the internet commentary on these studies, most of them are passed around to justify one viewpoint or another.
If the strongest statement you can make for your position is that there's no empirical evidence against the position, that's not much of a position.
key_insight: Despite strong ideological claims on both sides of the static-versus-dynamic typing debate, the actual empirical evidence is weak, methodologically flawed, and shows effects that are typically smaller than individual programmer variation—yet researchers and practitioners continue to cite these studies as definitive proof of their preferred position.
